coredata.dc:title: Preparation of edible antibacterial films based on corn starch /carbon nanodots for bioactive food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yuqing
Zhang, Junjun
Hu, Xuetao
Huang, Xiaowei
Zhang, Xinai
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
coredata.dc:description: Packaging plays an important role in protecting food from environmental impacts. However, traditional petroleum-based packaging has difficulty in meeting the antimicrobial and antioxidant requirements of prepared foods. This study introduced carbon dots (CDs), prepared by using carrot as a precursor, into corn starch (CS) to construct a bio-friendly composite film with high freshness retention properties. The scavenging of DPPH radicals reached 92.77 % at a CDs concentration of 512 µg/mL, and the antimicrobial activity of CS/5% CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was increased to 99.9 %. Notably, the homogeneous doping of CDs creates a dense surface and high carbon content inside the film, which promotes the elasticity and thermal stability of the composite film. Finally, we encapsulated deep-fried meatballs in CS-CDs films. The results showed that the CS-CDs films effectively protected the quality of deep-fried meatballs, and have excellent potential for application in food preservation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corn starch
Film
Antioxidant
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of CDs and films
Determination of free radical scavenging activities of CDs
Chroma values, thickness, and mechanical properties of films
Water vapor permeability (WVP) and thermal analysis of films
Application of CS-CDs films in deep-fried meatballs
The deep-fried meatballs preservation of the composite films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 32150410347, 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) Zeta potential of the CDs, (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs (insets: color of CDs in visible light and at 365 nm), (D) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (F) FT-IR spectra of CDs, (E) plausible mechanism of antioxidant activity of carbon dots, free radical scavenging activity of CDs towards (G) DPPH and (H) •OH (insets: absorbance spectra and color changes of mixture solutions at various concentrations of CDs).

(A) schematic illustration of the CDs synthesis and the CDs incorporated corn starch-based film preparation, SEM images for the surface morphology of (B) CS, (C) CS/1% CDs, (D) CS/2% CDs, (E) CS/3% CDs, (F) CS/4% CDs and (G) CS/5% CDs films. Atomic force microscopic cross-section profile of (H) CS, (I) CS/1% CDs, (J) CS/2% CDs, (K) CS/3% CDs, (L) CS/4% CDs and (M) CS/5% CDs films, (N) the Ra and Rq parameters of the films.

(A) Color comparison chart of composite films with different CDs doping ratios (including the percentage of CDs in the film-forming solution), (B) FT-IR spectra of synthesized films, (C) DSC profiles, (D) thickness, (E) WVP, (F) elongation at break and (G) tensile strength of composite films. Different letters represent that values have statistically significant differences from each other (p < 0.05).
(A) Effect of the prepared composite films on the growth of E. coli and S. aureus, effects of CDs content on inhibition efficiency of the composite films against (B) E. coli and (C) S. aureus.

(A) The image and (B) texture of fried meatballs without a package, packaged with CS, CS/1% CDs, CS/2% CDs, CS/3% CDs, CS/4% CDs and CS/5% CDs, during storage at 4 °C; (C) volatile organic compounds content, (D) PCA scatter plots and (E) radar chart of the fried meatballs in different treatment groups during storage analyzed by electronic nose.
Differential scanning calorimetry parameters of composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: T0: Onset, Tp: peak, Tc: end-set, ΔH: enthalpy. The films with pure chitosan, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt% and 5 wt% CDs concentrations (based on corn starch weight) were defined as CS, CS/1% CDs, CS/2% CDs, CS/3% CDs, CS/4% CDs and CS/5% CDs (0, 0.044, 0.08, 0.133, 0.17, 0.221 wt%).
All results are expressed as mean ± SD, n = 3. Different letters represent that values have statistically significant differences from each other (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Preparation of edible antibacterial films based on corn starch /carbon nanodots for bioactive food packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Packaging plays an important role in protecting food from environmental impacts. However, traditional petroleum-based packaging has difficulty in meeting the antimicrobial and antioxidant requirements of prepared foods. This study introduced carbon dots (CDs), prepared by using carrot as a precursor, into corn starch (CS) to construct a bio-friendly composite film with high freshness retention properties. The scavenging of DPPH radicals reached 92.77 % at a CDs concentration of 512 µg/mL, and the antimicrobial activity of CS/5% CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was increased to 99.9 %. Notably, the homogeneous doping of CDs creates a dense surface and high carbon content inside the film, which promotes the elasticity and thermal stability of the composite film. Finally, we encapsulated deep-fried meatballs in CS-CDs films. The results showed that the CS-CDs films effectively protected the quality of deep-fried meatballs, and have excellent potential for application in food preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of the prepared food market and increasing environmental awareness among consumers, bio-friendly food packaging is gaining attention in the market (Al-Tayyar, Youssef, & Al-hindi, 2020; Rydz, Musioł, Zawidlak-Węgrzyńska, & Sikorska, 2018). “Actively reinforced packaging film” is a new application-oriented material in the packaging sector which is an ideal alternative to petroleum-based and paper-based packaging (Roy, Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2022; Shen et al., 2023). Among them, packaging films with both antimicrobial and antioxidant activity, which slow down microbial reproduction and oxidative rancidity reactions by means of internal active fillers, thereby protecting food quality and prolonging shelf-life, have attracted a lot of attention (Purohit, Priyadarshi, Bhaskar, & Han, 2023). Currently, the main obstacles to the application of activity-enhanced packaging films in the field of prepared foods are the heat-induced biotoxicity of the polymer matrix and the impeded diffusion of large-sized active fillers in dense solid films. To circumvent these problems, the development of a bio-friendly packaging film encapsulates nanoscale active materials is needed.

Edible packaging has been recognized as an effective technology for circumventing the effects of potential bio-toxicity while protecting food from environmental factors (Ahmed, Santhosh, Thakur, Mulla, & Sarkar, 2023; Trajkovska Petkoska, Daniloski, D'Cunha, Naumovski, & Broach, 2021). The basic raw materials for edible packaging materials are based on natural edible substances that can be digested and absorbed by the human body, such as proteins, starches, polysaccharides, and plant fibres (Karimi Sani et al., 2023; Tian, Liu, Yang, & Wan, 2023). Among them, corn starch (CS) is a natural polysaccharide that can be extracted from the endosperm of corn kernels and has excellent film-forming ability, oxygen barrier and biodegradability (Zhang, Azizi-Lalabadi, Jafarzadeh, & Jafari, 2023). However, CS has the limitations of low thermal stability, high hydrophilicity and poor mechanical properties compared to traditional petroleum-based food packaging materials (M. Li et al., 2024; Srivastava, Singh, & Das, 2023). Notably, composites are often incorporated as active fillers in active packaging to simultaneously improve packaging physical properties and bioactivity. Unfortunately, the large size of the composites limits their movement in dense films (Zhao et al., 2023). Therefore, the preparation of a nanoscale size filler with efficient bioactivity has great potential for future food packaging.

Carbon dots (CDs) derived from food precursors have amazing qualities such as low-cost synthesis procedures and high yields (Liu et al., 2024; Rui et al., 2023). The CDs with extremely small particle size (<10 nm) can easily penetrate through dense solid films and lipid bilayers, leading to excellent antimicrobial activity (Rui et al., 2023). Previous studies have demonstrated that CDs exhibit amazing free radical scavenging ability, i.e., antioxidant properties, under the synergistic effect of electron transfer and surface functional groups (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Notably, carbon precursors are one of the important factors affecting the performance of CDs. For example, Xu et al. designed and synthesized red date-derived J-CDs to specifically promote erythrocyte proliferation by stimulating self-renewal of erythroid progenitor cells (Xu et al., 2022). Tong et al. synthesized a highly biocompatible CDs by hydrothermal method using glycyrrhizic acid as a raw material to inhibit porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus at a high level (Tong et al., 2020). Inspired by these studies, we used carrots with antioxidant properties as a green carbon source for the preparation of CDs with effective reactive oxygen species eradication properties. Furthermore, CDs made from natural food as a carbon source have excellent biocompatibility and minimal biotoxicity, and have shown good edible properties as fillers for packaging films (Hatimuria et al., 2023; Raja et al., 2019; S.-T. Yang et al., 2009).

In this study, biologically active CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using carrot as a precursor. Subsequently, the synthesized CDs were doped into CS matrix as nanofillers with different ratios to obtain a series of biologically active and controllable functional films. To verify the antioxidant activity of CDs, the scavenging rate of DPPH radicals by different concentrations of CDs was explored. In addition, the antimicrobial properties against Escherichia coli (E. coil) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were obtained based on the evaluation of the plate counting method under constant temperature and humidity incubation conditions. Finally, the performance of CS-CDs composite films for practical applications in high oil environments and their effects on food color, flavor and texture were investigated using deep-fried meatballs as an analytical model. In summary, the aim of this study was to investigate the effects of CDs addition on the physicochemical properties, antioxidant activity and antimicrobial effect of CS films, and to elucidate the preservation mechanism of CS-CDs composite films.

CS was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Carrots were purchased from a local supermarket. Glycerol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nutrient agar and nutrient broth were purchased from Bkmam Biotechnology Co. E. coli and S. aureus strains were obtained from Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center. Ethanol, glycerol, and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazine (DPPH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.

CDs were synthesized hydrothermally using carrots as the precursor, according to previous method (Wen, Li, Yan, & Su, 2023). Briefly, 20 g of carrots were placed in a reactor containing ethanol (15 mL) and ultrapure water (20 mL), which was subsequently heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. After that, dialyzed for 24 h with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da, and the CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

Solution casting technique was used to create films made of cornstarch. 5 g of CS, 2.5 mL (3.15 g, 25 °C) of glycerol and 100 mL of distilled water were mixed and heated in a boiling water bath for 40 min while stirring continuously. After cooling the starch slurry to 60 °C, 5 mL of CDs dispersion (dissolved in ultrapure water) was added to obtain film-forming solution with different CDs proportions. 5 mL of purified water was inserted to the blank control. The films with pure chitosan, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt% and 5 wt% CDs concentrations (based on CS weight) were defined as CS, CS/1% CDs, CS/2% CDs, CS/3% CDs, CS/4% CDs and CS/5% CDs. Correspondingly, the final percentage concentrations of CDs in the film-forming solution were 0 wt%, 0.044 wt%, 0.08 wt%, 0.133 wt%, 0.17 wt%, and 0.221 wt%. The final percentage concentration of CS was 4.42 wt% and the final percentage concentration of glycerol was 2.78 wt%. 18 mL of film-forming solution was poured on round mold with a diameter of 9 cm, and placed in an oven at 35 °C for 8 h to form the films eventually. The films were conditioned in a Constant Temperature and Humidity Incubator (Ningbo Jiangnan Instrument Factory, China) at 75 % relative humidity (RH) for 48 h and then sealed for storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of CDs and films
ce:section.ce:para: The surface groups were characterized by the WQF-510A Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Rayleigh Ltd., China). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were analyzed using Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) and F97XP spectrometer (Lengguang technology, Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-1400, JEOL, Japan). The potential was analyzed using the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern, Malvern, UK). A variable vacuum tungsten wire scanning electron microscope (SEM) (S-7001, Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan) was used to take surface and cross-sectional pictures of the films.

The surface structure of the film was characterized by an atomic force microscope (Multimode 8, Bruker Corporation, USA), and the roughness of the film was measured. Gwyddion software was used to draw the image, and the film transverse profile was taken to observe its thickness distribution. Parameters such as mean surface roughness (Ra) and root mean square surface roughness (Rq) were calculated by NanoScope Analysis software. The surface morphology of films was evaluated comprehensively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of free radical scavenging activities of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The first step was to perform the DPPH radical scavenging assay (Murru, Badía-Laíño, & Díaz-García, 2020). Different masses of CDs powder were dissolved in 1 L of ultrapure water and the CDs solution was obtained after sonication for 15 min. Different concentrations (1–512 μg/mL) of CDs solution and 1 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH ethanol solution were reacted avoiding the light for 20 min and measure absorbances (517 nm). The DPPH scavenging activity was computed using the following equation:
Scavenging
c a p a i t y
( % )
=
1 -
A i
A 0
× 100 %
where Ai and A0 were the absorbance of the mixture with CDs and without CDs, respectively.

The Fenton reaction was used to determine the scavenging activity of CDs against hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (Huang, Lin, Zhang, Yan, Wang, & Liu, 2019). 500 μL of FeSO4 solution (9 mM) and 500 μL of ethanol salicylic acid solution (9 mM) were mixed with 100 μL of CDs at different concentrations (0.016–4.096 mg/mL). 5 mL of H2O2 (800 mM) was added to the mixture and the absorbance at 510 nm was measured after 15 min of dark reaction. The •OH scavenging activity was calculated using the same equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chroma values, thickness, and mechanical properties of films
ce:section.ce:para:
L*, a* and b* of the film (30 × 10 mm) were measured utilizing a colorimeter (CM-2300d, Konica Minolta, Japan), five measurements were taken as parallel groups (using a control whiteboard as a calibration reference). The thickness was determined using a digital helical micrometer and measured 5 times as a parallel group (MDC-25SX, Mitutoyo, China). The elongation at break (EB) and tensile strength (TS) of the films (30 × 10 mm) were researched employing a texture analyzer (TA.XT Plus, Stable Micro Systems, UK) (Z. Yang et al., 2023). Before testing, the distance between the upper and lower fixtures was adjusted to 20 mm and the crosshead speed was configured as 0.06 m/s. The EB and TS values of the films were calculated using the following equations:
EB =
L -
L 0
L 0
× 100 %
TS =
F S

where L0 was the initial distance between the fixtures before the test (mm), and L was the length between the fixtures at the time of sample fracture (mm). F was the maximum force (N) applied to the film at the time of fracture, and S was the initial cross-sectional area of the sample (m2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and thermal analysis of films
ce:section.ce:para:
25 mL of ultrapure water was poured into a 50 mL centrifuge tube (27.3 mm internal diameter). Fixed the film (90 mm diameter circle) firmly to the orifice of the 50 mL centrifuge tube (both circles overlap). The gap between the water and the film was 56 mm. Measured the weight of the centrifuge tube every 12 h until the weight remained constant. The tubes were then weighed every 2 h for the next 48 h (three tests per film sample). The WVP was calculated using the following formula:
WVP =
Δ m ± h
S × T × Δ p

where Δm was the weight change (g), and h was the thickness of the film sample (mm). S was the water vapor transmission area (m2). T was the time (s), and Δp is the difference in water vapor pressure between the two sides of the film sample (Pa).

The thermal stability of each film sample was evaluated using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 3, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). A film sample of 4 mg mass was heated from 30 °C to 200 °C (under nitrogen atmosphere) at a constant rate of 10 °C/min.

Antimicrobial activity of film samples was tested against Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogens represented by E. coli and S. aureus. 5 mL of the bacterial solution (109 CFU/mL) was dispensed into a centrifuge tube containing 50 mg of film solution. 100 μL of the strain dilution solution was spread evenly on nutrient agar plates and incubated at 36 °C for 18 to 36 h. The expansion of colonies was tracked and watched. The growth inhibition rate was then calculated based on the number of colonies.
Growthinhibition =
N 0
- N
N 0
× 100 %
where N0 and N are the number of colonies without and with film solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CS-CDs films in deep-fried meatballs
ce:section.ce:para: Deep-fried meatballs were chosen as the detection model to verify the performance of the prepared composite films. The recipe for meatballs was modified according to Zun Wang et al (Z. Wang, Ng, Warner, Stockmann, & Fang, 2023). The fat and lean meat were separated from each other in the supermarket in Jiangsu, China, and the mass ratio of lean meat to fat meat was 7:3, and the meat was minced in a meat grinder. According to the traditional process, 7 g of salt, 50 g of starch, 3 g of pepper, 10 g of ginger juice, 2 eggs and 30 mL of soya sauce (Haitian Group) were added to each 500 g of fresh pork, mixed well and then stored at 4 °C for 1 h. Finally, the meatballs, each weighing 30 g, were manually made and deep-fried at 180 °C for 5 min. The film was coated after the deep-fried meatballs had cooled to room temperature for subsequent testing.
Chroma values and mechanical properties of coated and uncoated deep-fried meatballs were measured according to 2.6.

A PEN3 electronic nose (E-nose) system (Airsense Analytics GmbH, Schwerin, Germany) was utilized to perform odor analysis. The system contained 10 metal oxide single thick film sensors that provide an odor fingerprint of the sample. The performance characteristics of the E-nose gas sensor array were detailed in Table S1 (Yu, Huang, Wang, Ren, Zhang, & Wang, 2022).

To generate a specific number of volatile compounds in the headspace, 3 g of deep-fried meatballs (including film) were weighed into a 10 mL headspace vial. For preliminary experiments, the rinse time was set to 310 s, the measurement time to 200 s, and the gas flow rate to 25 mL/min. After each measurement, the gas sensor was cleaned with clean air in preparation for the next sample measurement. To maintain the electronic nose's stability, the detection temperature of all samples was kept at 25 ± 1 °C. Samples were analyzed in triplicate for better parallelism (H. Tian et al., 2023).

Significant differences in the experimental results were assessed using non-parametric tests due to the assumption of non-normality. The Kruskal-Wallis test, followed by Dunn's post hoc test for multiple comparisons, was employed for the data analysis. The experimental result data were processed using SPSS 25.0. Statistical significance was considered at a level of p < 0.05.

Fig. 1 provided insights into the microstructure, fluorescence properties, optical properties and antioxidant capacity of bio-based CDs derived from CS. The shape and size of the CDs (Fig. 1A) were similar to those previously reported, being roughly spherical and well dispersed (G. Li, Liu, Zhang, Zhai, Lai, & Jiang, 2023).

Furthermore, the surface charge of the sample was determined by measuring its zeta potential. The surface charges of particles with high zeta potentials tended to repel each other, stabilize the dispersion, and maintain functionality. Absolute values far from zero meat higher stability of the solution. The zeta potential of the prepared CDs was approximately −12.16 mV. This suggested that electrostatic repulsion between the charged functional groups may help to stabilize the CDs.

The synthesized CD was brown in visible bands but blue when irradiated by UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1C). The prepared CDs showed a conspicuous UV–visible absorption peak at 295 nm (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D revealed the effect of excitation wavelength on the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, the emission intensity depends on the excitation wavelength and exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior.

To verify the functional groups existing in the nanomaterials and investigate their ability to scavenge free radicals, FTIR analysis was conducted. The FTIR spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 1F) revealed several absorption peaks that corresponded to specific functional groups. The absorption peak observed at 3048 cm−1 indicated the presence of NH stretching vibrations, while the peak at 2989 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of aliphatic CH bonds. The absorption peak at 1409 cm−1 was assigned to the amide CO stretching frequency, indicating the presence of carboxyl groups (COOH) in the CDs. Additionally, the peak at 1042 cm−1 was associated with the CO stretching vibration. These electron-donating functional groups reduce free radicals by participating in the formation of adducts after free electrons leave the domain following proton donation (Fig. 1E).

From the results shown in Fig. 1G, the absorption intensity of DPPH radicals at 517 nm decreased when the carbon dot concentration was increased from 0 µg/mL to 512 µg/mL, indicating a dose-dependent scavenging ability of CDs for DPPH radicals. As the concentration of CDs was increased from 1 µg/mL to 512 µg/mL, the scavenging of DPPH radicals by CDs increased from 0.47 % to 92.77 %, and the color of DPPH ethanol solution changed from dark purple to yellow (Fig. 1G). This excellent DPPH scavenging ability was likely due to the presence of amounts of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs (mainly hydroxyl, which was a hydrogen donor group). Fig. 1E presented the scavenging mechanism of CDs for DPPH radicals, when contacted with hydrogen, generate stable DPPH-H complexes, leading to color fading of the DPPH solution. As shown in Fig. 1H, the absorbance of the mixed solution decreased gradually as the concentration of CDs was increased from 0 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL, indicating that the scavenging of free radicals by CDs increased gradually. A possible scavenging mechanism of CDs on free radicals was proposed in Fig. 1E, where oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs can contribute hydrogen atoms to react with the released free radicals to produce stable complexes that change the color of the solution from purple to yellow (Fig. 1H). Furthermore, CDs showed stronger resistance to DPPH radicals compared to ε-polylysine and curcumin. Therefore, the application of CS-CDs films is a good choice for controlling the oxidation rate of food products (Y. Tian et al., 2023; Yong, Gu, Nabeel Ahmad, Wang, Wang, & Zhu, 2024).

The surface dispersion of nanoparticles on pure CS films and CS-CDs composite films was examined using SEM. The film fabrication process was illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The surface of the pure CS films exhibited a uniform morphology (Fig. 2B), with no visible voids or cracks, although it appeared slightly rough. With the addition of CDs ranging from 0 to 2 % (Fig. 2D), the films became denser, more uniform, and smoother, without any voids or cracks. The CDs were evenly dispersed within the CS-CDs composite films. However, at a CD addition of 3 % (Fig. 2E), the film became inhomogeneous, and voids and cracks began to appear, likely due to the aggregation of CD particles, which increased the surface roughness of the film. When the addition of CDs increased from 4 % (Fig. 2F) to 5 % (Fig. 2G), the voids and cracks on the surface of the films were exacerbated because the rise in the concentration of CDs increased the particle agglomeration phenomenon. These results indicated that at low CD concentrations, the CDs were uniformly distributed within the CS matrix, resulting in well-distributed and compatible film components.

The cross-section profile of the AFM was shown in Fig. 2H to Fig. 2M. The addition of low concentration CDs can reduce the roughness of the film. When the carbon dot concentration was 2 %, the film surface roughness was the lowest. When the concentration exceeds 2 %, the agglomeration of CDs affected the surface roughness of the film, which was consistent with the results of SEM. Furthermore, a quantitative analysis was performed on the arithmetic mean roughness deviation of the film surface from the mean line (Ra) and root mean square roughness (Rq) parameters. Ra and Rq progressively dropped and the film roughness decreased when less than 2 % CDs was applied, as seen in Fig. 2N. However, the addition of CDs at concentrations higher than 2 % resulted in a progressive increase in both Ra and Rq as well as an increase in film roughness.

The compounds included in the films were analyzed for their functional groups and their interactions with one another using FT-IR (Fig. 3 A). The tensile vibration of amino groups was reflected by the broad peak seen at about 3430 cm−1. The presence of CH stretching vibration in the chitosan ring was signified by the peak observed at 2925 cm−1. The carbonyl group's bending vibration was identified as the cause of the peak at 1648 cm−1, which suggested the presence of N-acetyl groups in the maize starch. The amide's NH bending was shown by the absorption peak at 1570 cm−1. Furthermore, the CO stretching vibration was represented by the peak at 1042 cm−1, whereas the absorption band at 1147 cm−1 matched the tensile vibration given to CO. The FT-IR spectrum of the CS-CDs composite films showed no discernible wavelength shift and was comparable to that of CS. This implied that the CD addition had no effect on the CS film's general structure. Non-covalent and hydrogen-bonded interactions between the CDs and the maize starch matrix may be the cause of the small elevation in spectral intensity observed in the films with more CDs.

Table 1 and Fig. 3B showed the DSC curves for CS and CDs/CS films, respectively. The components of the polymer were identified using DSC analysis. The endothermic peak of the CS and composite film was ascribed to the vaporization of bound and absorbed water, whereas the CS film showed a maximum peak at roughly 100 °C. With the addition of CDs, there were slight changes in the start, peak, and end temperatures. The ΔH of the film increased with CDs enhancement. The observation results showed that CS and CDs have good thermal stability.

Reducing moisture loss was an important indicator for food packaging. The WVP of a film reflected its water barrier effect (Fig. 3D). It was clear that when the concentration of CDs was less than 2 %, the WVP value decreased with the addition of CDs. Similarly, 2 % composite films exhibited excellent water vapor performance compared to CS films. However, the WVP increased with the increase in the content of CDs when the content of CDs was more than 2 %. These changes could be ascribed to the fact that the addition of low concentrations of CD made the films more compact and densely structured. As a result, the passage of water vapor through the pores of film became notably challenging, leading to a significant enhancement in the film's gas barrier properties. However, when agglomeration occurs with increased concentration of CDs, the appearance of gaps on the surface of the film cannot reduce water loss. Therefore, composite films with low concentration of CDs can be chosen to meet the moisture requirements of different food packaging applications.

17 mL of various film-forming solutions were uniformly cast into a mold measuring 9 cm × 9 cm. The thickness was measured after drying and film formation (Fig. 3C). After a small addition of added CDs (1 %), the thickness of the composite film is lower than that of the pure CS film, which is attributed to the fact that the CDs within the film will have a strong hydrogen-bonding effect with the molecular chains of the CS to form a denser cross-linked network. With the addition of more and more CDs, the thickness of the film continued to increase, which may be due to the hydrogen bonding breakage between CDs and CS as well as the self-agglomeration of CDs. Fig. 3E and Fig. 3F show the changes in EB and TS of the films after the addition of different amounts of CDs (0–5 %), respectively. The composite films doped with small amounts of CDs exhibited better mechanical properties compared to the CS films without added CDs. When the content of CDs reached 2 %, the TS and EB of the composite films increased significantly, reflecting the plasticizing ability of CDs. The increase in mechanical properties was attributed to the denser film structure due to the hydrogen bonding force between CDs and CS. However, when the content of CDs was further increased, the films exhibited a decrease in mechanical properties, which could be attributed to agglomeration induced by the high concentration of CDs (Wang et al., 2022).

The antibacterial effect of the composite films against E. coli (Gram-negative bacterium) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterium) was illustrated in Fig. 4 . Comparing the antimicrobial effects of different composite films in Fig. 4A, it was evident that the concentration of CDs significantly enhanced the antimicrobial activity of the composite films. Specifically, the antimicrobial activity of CS/ 5 % CDs reaches up to 99.9 % (Fig. 4B and Fig. 4C). The increased antibacterial activity of the films can be attributed to the properties of the active filler CDs. The cell membrane of bacteria consists of amphiphilic peptidoglycan and phospholipid molecular layers, and the small size and high specific surface area of CDs contribute to their dynamic residence in the biofilm, affecting bacterial division and proliferation. Moreover, some CDs are absorbed into the interior of the bacteria and alter the secondary structure of the genetic material through non-covalent binding, thus affecting the genetic process of the bacteria (Varghese & Balachandran, 2021).

Currently, many studies on films doped with active fillers to increase the antimicrobial properties have been reported. Li et al. showed strong antimicrobial properties (89.2 % clearing of E. coli and 93.4 % clearing of S. aureus) by nanosilver doped films, which was similar to the results of this study. However, the preparation of AgO/Ag/CGO films was too cumbersome, limiting their application in the food packaging (C. Li, Wang, Li, & Xie, 2024). Da Silva et al. used grape pomace extract as an active filler and the antimicrobial rate was only about 50 %, which was due to the large size of the extract leading to a reduction in the mobility efficiency and hence the antimicrobial activity (da Silva, de Oliveira, Wang, Carastan, & Rosa, 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The deep-fried meatballs preservation of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Deep-fried meatballs wrapped in CS films containing CDs were stored at 4 °C for 24 h and evaluated for changes in meat appearance, texture and flavor. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the surface color of fried meatballs wrapped in pure CS became lighter, and the difference between fried meatballs wrapped in CS doped with CDs and the control could not be seen with the naked eye.

Textural properties are important attributes that affect the quality and swallowing characteristics of book review ampoules, and the physical property analyzer TPA to simulate the chewing behavior of the oral cavity can be used to accurately determine the textural characteristic indexes such as hardness, resilience, springiness and cohesion of the food. Fig. 5B shows the textural parameters of deep-fried meatballs coated with different coating films. The hardness of the coated meatballs increased in all cases compared to the control (uncoated deep-fried meatballs), but decreased slightly with the increase of carbon point concentration. Hardness is one of the main parameters of textural properties that respond to the mouthfeel of the food, representing the force required to break the food during chewing, and also directly affects the springiness, resilience, and cohesion to a large extent. Compared with the uncoated meatballs, the film-coated wrapped meatballs showed an increase in resilience and elasticity, which may be related to the internal hydrogen bonding of the CS film. The above results indicated that the surface coating slightly enhanced the chewing properties of the fried meatballs.

Ten sensors (i.e. R1, R2…R10) in the electronic nose system played an important role in the differentiation of the fried meatball samples (Fig. 5C, Fig. 5D and Fig. 5E). Sensors R2, R6, R7 and R8 showed more pronounced variations throughout the assay, suggesting a greater variation in the changes of nitrogen–oxygen, sulfide, ethanol and aromatic volatiles during storage of the fried meatballs (Fig. 5C, E), whereas the remaining six sensors showed almost no changes. Based on the data derived from the electronic nose analysis, PCA effectively separated the odors of the fried meatball samples under different coating wrappings (Fig. 5D). There were some overlapping areas between all the samples, indicating that the odors of these samples were similar and not significantly different. It can be surmised that the wrapping of CS films with different carbon point concentrations did not alter the odors of the deep-fried meatball samples.

In current study, carrot CDs were leveraged to enhance the physical mechanical properties of CS-based films and to prepare composite films with antioxidant activity and antimicrobial activity. Relative to the CS films, the films with the addition of low concentration of CDs had better solubility, WVP, TS, EB and heat resistance. In addition, the composite films had good techno-functional properties such as antioxidant and antimicrobial. These films fulfilled the necessary physical and chemical requirements for high quality packaging materials. When applied to the storage and preservation of fried meatballs, these films effectively inhibited the growth of microorganisms, thus maintaining the appearance, odor and quality of the meatballs. In summary, CS-CDs composite film is an effective and stable active packaging material for food preservation with great potential to replace petroleum-based packaging.

Yuqing Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Junjun Zhang: Data curation, Software, Validation. Xuetao Hu: Formal analysis. Xiaowei Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Xinai Zhang: Investigation. Xiaobo Zou: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Resources, Validation. Jiyong Shi: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Resources, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 32150410347, 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138467.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
